speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five of the Survivors of the Shoah interview with Henry Frankel. So you were telling us about meeting up with--

speaker: interviewee
text: The experience of working in Majdanek and Lublin and approached by the Polish man who was slowly preparing me for the escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name. And I did not ask for a name. He just was calling me by my first name, by the name of [? Tadik. ?] And when I-- this was going on for about two or three weeks, until one day he told me that we can use the outside bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going on for a few weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going on for a few weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: During this few weeks, we were-- he was explaining to me that there is possibility of escape through the-- using the outdoor-- outdoor bathroom. And there was a very primitive [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] closure-- closure with-- you had to stand on-- over opening in the ground with a few planks of wood that you had to sit down and release yourself. But he mentioned that behind this was right there-- tracks of the railroads passing by at the same time. And the trick was to cross-- to get through the opening in the ground and get from underneath the wire-- the barbed wire that was surrounding the encampment of this factory-- and start running toward the train that was approaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now every day--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day you were working in the munitions factory at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, we were returning back to-- to camp. We were brought by truck in the morning. And at night, we were marching from the factory back to the camp. And they were counted very carefully by the Germans if the amount of these prisoners the same as had left in the morning. And he was explaining to me that around 4:00 in the afternoon, there is always a train passing towards the north part of the railroad tracks. And this is the train to jump on [INAUDIBLE] if I would successfully cross underneath of the outdoor restrooms. And I said, I have a striped uniform. They will recognize me very easily, no matter where I will go. He said on the day that we-- you will decide that you have to go, you will have a hang-- hanging different coat and different pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number at this point also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had the number of-- mine was 14,284.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it on your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my uniform on my chest, and on the side of the pants, with the two triangle. One was red. And the other one was yellow. The red was political. And the yellow was the Jew. But there was uniform of Star of David, so they were pinning on every Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they know you were political?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not starting to do political. You were a Jew. And that's why you became political to them. Did not necessarily you had to be political. Every Jew was wearing red and yellow star. And that's how they could tell the Jew from the Gentile. And the orders were having red-- the Polish people, Czech people, whoever came from other country to Majdanek-- get the red triangle. The larger part on top, and the triangle on the bottom, narrow part. And above this was the number. And the same thing was on the left side of the pant. And this-- with a uniform like this, you couldn't get very far in Poland Somebody would have recognized you and turn you in. Or the Germans would recognize you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you needed some other outfit. And he simply said to me, there will be a little bit bigger than you can stand. But don't change it in the bathroom. Carry it over your head or underneath. But you have to be quick, responsive, and we have faith that you can do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was fortunate that I did not have the clog shoes-- the wooden shoes like Dutch shoes. But the regular shoes. And with the shoes like this, I could move much faster much better, and be more mobile. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other people escaping at this time? Did you know of anybody else who was?

speaker: interviewee
text: If there were, but not through the toilets. Because there was always somebody assisting you to the toilet. And they would stand against some distance. But you were not free to go to toilet and return. There was always one of the Germans from the SS watching or responding. They were close place with a little door so there was little privacy. And that's how they decided. It was also used by some other groups who came to the munition factory to work. And among them was some Polack. If their other facility were closed, they came to this place and release themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the prisoner went to release themselves, their SS man escorted them. I was fortunate enough that nobody went with me except me. And there was just the elderly SS man standing outside with a gun on his hip. And I don't remember if he had like a gun open in the holster or not. But I decided that if I'm told to do it, I will do it this very day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was about three weeks before Christmas when the beginning of December that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --decision was made.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. The decision was made by this Polish man that I will undertake the escape. And I went in to this place with a heavy heart. But I know that there is a better way to do it than live in a camp. So you have to take a chance and really try to escape. And this was the day that I could really glorify that I managed to-- with all this things, with all this release from other people, I have managed to crawl out on the other side and drill like hell. And the SS man knocking on the door, wondering why the taking so long. And then they-- then they have no response. So he took out the gun from his holster and start shooting. And I got hit twice-- once in arm. And there is evidence still existing. The bullet went right to my body, through my arm and through my-- got stuck in the upper arm. And I somehow managed to get to the train and jump and hold on with the left hand and pull myself on the steps of the train. And a-- after about some period of time, I'd lost my consciousness for loss of blood. I fell off the train. And a Polish farmer found me. And he knew quite well that I managed to put on the coat and the pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: You change your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You changed your clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But he had seen from underneath these clothes there were the striped uniform of a prisoner. So. And I was laying in the snow. There was snowy day. And they picked me up and brought me to the farm. And he was taking care of me for about two or three-- two or three days. And maybe a little longer. I really can't tell, because I lost completely [INAUDIBLE] the terms of time. But I know that one day he came up, he said, that you are strong enough to walk. And I have to bring you back to Warsaw. I didn't ask too many questions. And it was about an hour and a half ride to the outskirts of the city Warsaw, on the other side of-- from the Prague. Warsaw has two parts-- the main Warsaw and the Praga, Praga. And then we managed to get through the streets of Warsaw until we got to the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] bridge of Kierbedzia and connecting the two parts of Warsaw. I was on the sacks of bad potatoes that was frozen. And I somehow managed to-- to survive under these potatoes. And when the Germans stop this farmer, ask him what he has and they smell the potatoes, they wave him through. And we got to the other part of the Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the other side, the same story. The German asked, what you have? He said, I have bad potatoes. So he said, I can smell it. Go on, go on. And make sure that you disappear. Don't smell up the whole city of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought me to a place that he knew that I have to get out. I told him where. And there was farther street. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot the name of the street. And there was a friend of my father who was living there. And I-- somehow he picked me up. It was around 5 o'clock It was getting a little bit dark. And he helped me out from underneath the sacks of potatoes. And you can imagine how I smelled when I-- I got out from underneath this thing for about two hours of riding under. And I came through the gently knock on door of the address that was given to me. And I knew it. The man came out. He said, oh, God. I can't keep you over here. You better go upstairs through the attic. I will see you in a short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So with the staircase-- it was a five-story house-- I quickly managed to get to the attic. And the attic was very cold without them heating it. I was shivering like a fish and trying to get rid of the smell of the potatoes, and they soaked the uniform and soaked the clothing. And I was sitting barely [INAUDIBLE] waiting for something to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the man came with a piece of bread and some milk. And I gulp it down. And then he had a towel. And he-- a large towel. And I wiped myself, and somehow managed to regain my composure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the farmer's name was, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I do. But I don't know. Benarick I think. I heard somebody calling him the name Benarick And-- but I really am not positive about the name, because when I went to Poland the first time in 1988, I was looking for any such name. And on the outskirts [BACKROUND NOISE] of the [? Praga ?] [? City ?] to. And they told me that they didn't know or didn't see or heard of any such name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what town you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I know that I was very close to the city of Otwock, spelled O-T-W-O-C-K. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because when we were riding from the farmer's farm house to Praga, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the farmer said something about Otwock. I did remember that they did this one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he want to take you back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to get back to-- to the ghetto. I didn't know-- I did hope that perhaps this friend of my father and a man that I knew from before the war would be of some help to me. But since he refused to give me any help except providing some clothing and-- and some help of contacting people to get me through the sewer lines, that's all what he could do. So he told me what he going to do and how that's-- how would it be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosnowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-S-N-O-W-S-K-I. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why do you think the resistance helped you escape? Why do you think the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were trying to get anybody who was capable of getting out-- out of the camps to give them another chance in life, I guess. Or maybe what the work that I'd done before for them for distributing the leaflets and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you to go anywhere after you escaped? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. To the contrary, they told me that you have to realize that we only can get you out on this [INAUDIBLE]. But then you will be on-- all on your own. And I accepted-- I thought perhaps if I wouldn't have been shot, I probably would have gone into the woods and joined the partisans. But due to the fact that I was shot, I just lost consciousness. Coincidental fact that I did survive escaping from Majdanek gave me enough courage to continue going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you seriously wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer asked me if there is any place in Warsaw that he want to deliver-- to bring me. And I mentioned this place of-- and father's name-- father's friend's name Sosnowski. And this Sosnowski, he evidently was connected with some people who had underground activities or-- or knowledge, because two days later, after sleeping for two solid days, and just having minimum food to share with and a warm bowl of good soup that I will never forget in my life, two men came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosnowski came to me and said, tomorrow morning at 5 o'clock I will wake you up. Be prepared to leave immediately. And I said, where am I going? What are you going to do? So he said there was somebody pick you up. And-- What condition were you in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already be able to move around. And I recovered quite quickly from the-- from the shots and so on. And then I was thin. I was undernourished. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not still not looking like an old Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they took a look at me, they said, how are you gonna make it through the sewer? Two men came at 5 o'clock exactly. And they didn't ask too many thing. They had-- do you know what a honey well is? There's a wagon picking up the residue from the toilets. And they came with such wagon-- horse-driven, not motor-driven. And they brought me to a place where there was an opening for the sewer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And away from the house of Sosnowski to the place where there was the opening to the canal. They explained to me how many-- how long do I have to walk in the canals, and what the canals are like, and so on. And they gave me quite a few handkerchiefs and a bottle of water. They said, just follow our instructions. And you will survive the canals. One other simple thing. You have to-- the gases from the canals-- they were sewage canals, and built quite a long time ago. And you had to know and count to be totally aware that you-- where you are located and how long you have to exist and go on. And I somehow managed to follow the instruction and keep changing the handkerchief with water, because the smell and the gasses from the residues are unbelievable. You really can go out of your mind. You can lose your consciousness, even. And you are-- were not prepared to deal with. But the most important thing is that I counted very exactly the passages or the additional arteries joining it. And when I got to the final one, I knew that this is the place that I would be ghetto. And when I climbed out, it was not be easy to lift up for me this plate from the canal. And somehow evidently people passing by outside had seen some movement. And they lifted it up with me. And I crawled-- they pulled me out out of there. I close this up. I knew I was in ghetto. And when I was in ghetto, it was about-- I would say about 200 yards from the junction of [INAUDIBLE] and-- and Zamenhof. But already on the Jew-- on the ghetto side.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: When? When is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was today where there is a monument standing to the heroes of the Warsaw ghetto. There was a-- a place where the Jewish community of the small ghetto was existing. Not far away from there was a street called Wolynska, spelled W-O-L-Y-N-S-K-A. And that's where I got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between that little place and [INAUDIBLE] was the street Wolynska.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when is this that you came back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was three days before-- [BACKROUND NOISE] be three days before Christmas. It was on the 21st or 22nd of December of 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people from the organization for the movement recognized me. And they said, where did you come from? And I say, I can't talk to nobody else but [INAUDIBLE]. They said, why do you want to see [INAUDIBLE]? And I explained to them that it's very important. I'm back from Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they heard Majdanek, they put the warm-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] sort of caftans over me. They noticed I was wet and smelled again like a piece of [?shit?]. And they rushed me to [INAUDIBLE]. And that's where when my-- when-- Mordechai had seen me, he started to cry. And then one of the girls, [INAUDIBLE], started to run undress me. And somebody gave me a warm glass of tea. And then I somehow relaxed and slowly after changing clothes and being washed a little bit, fell asleep. And I slept for about two days, until there was something going on the 1st of January. And [INAUDIBLE] came over. And he said, well, tell me the story. And this is exactly what I told [INAUDIBLE]. He said, well, congratulations. But I want you to know you're not the only one who escaped from Majdanek. And I said, I'm very proud that I'm not the only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he started to talk to me about the camp and the functions and the buildings and what's going on with the crematoriums and the crematorium. He was very much interested in how many people were in there and who was in there. And on the 2nd or 3rd of January he came over to me. And he said, well, you are one of the trusted members of Hashomer Hatzair. I want you to know that we are planning something. Are you willing to join us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, of course. I'm old hand at it. And on January 6, 1943, [INAUDIBLE] woke me up. And he said, the day of action had arrived. And there were 360 people lured by the Germans with some additional food and bread and marmalade and so on to be transported [INAUDIBLE] not transported, but to be escorted to Umschlagplatz. And [INAUDIBLE] decided that's enough of this nonsense of bringing people to Umschlagplatz. And let's attack the Germans and release the people. And there were six out of the group of the 360. We disperse 300 people, at least. It was not a simple thing, because there was the first encounter of underground with Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite well organized, because youngsters were-- we did not have any special weapons. And the weapons that we had could not be totally used for the purpose [BACKROUND NOISE] of freeing so many people. But we-- they were using stones and youngsters primarily to hit the helmets of the escorting Germans. There were about 18 or 20 Germans with the guns in their hands. But with heavy tunic and helmets and escorting them from the [INAUDIBLE] Street to the Umschlagplatz

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with the group they were escorting?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was with the group of [INAUDIBLE] waiting for to attack the people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew where they were going to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- we-- we knew that they started to move the people from the little place on the-- in front of the Jewish community to Zamenhof Street to they assembly this group very early in the morning. And around 9 o'clock, they started to move the people around. When the Germans were hit with-- on the helmet with a stone, they dropped the guns. And many of our boys were just waiting for the moment. And they picked up the guns and pointed at the Germans. Of course, they started to scream, don't shoot! Don't shoot! I didn't do nothing wrong. And we took off the tunic-- their coats-- their tunic, their ammunition, their shoes, and let them-- left them running in their underwear. And whoever-- whichever was of them hit and hit badly was by the approaching Ukrainian unit attached to the SS for the purposes of the [? traditional ?] Warsaw ghetto. And of the 360-- the 360 people, there were 300 dispersed and placed in bunkers and other locations. The other 60 were frozen stiff in a place they couldn't move. You wouldn't do anything with them. And the Ukrainians picked them up and brought them to Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any shots fired?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- they shoot-- many shots fired. And the ones we had weapons in our hands, we had the upper hand. And we were trying to beat the Ukrainians out. But more units came from behind, from the [INAUDIBLE] street, a gate, and they joined the others and they succeeded to take the 60 people to Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewee
text: How many were killed? A thing like this, we don't know. Because immediately after the 300 people were safe in the gates of houses and ruins of the ghetto, which was slowly devastated by the Germans, we managed to-- to escape. And the prize was an enormous amount of weapon they had gotten from the Germans' hands when they were hit by the stones. And I guess it must have been some impact and to be on a-- to be hit by a stone on the helmet to the point that you drop the gun. And you get to your knees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans were not easily to succeed. But once we noticed that their guns were on the ground, that they were holding on to their heads, we knew that we could succeed of getting the weapons from them.
